## Source: amazon-inspector (a6212e535568abe842341e0c1ddc4548ac0146e1bbce874e0a45d282f4d196a1)
Package is published at version 99.99.99 under the @doaction/* scope — the canonical version-bump shape used to override private/internal scope resolution and force public registry to win. package.json declares preinstall = 'node scripts/postinstall.js', which require()'s '@doaction/shared/bin/preinstall.js' from a sibling dependency pinned at ^99.99.99. The sibling is not shipped in the tarball; npm fetches and executes it on every install. The package's own source (src/index.js line 9 imports collectEnv/sendToDatadog/reportEnvToDatadog from @doaction/shared; scripts/preinstall.js line 4 comment 'Triggers safe environment telemetry on npm install') documents that the install-time payload reads the installer's process environment and forwards it to Datadog under service tag 'doaction-pay'. Installer environments commonly contain CI tokens, cloud credentials, and registry auth — these leave the machine on `npm install` with no user consent and no opt-out.

## Source: ghsa-malware (ea0e4224fea42b723c5ddeed073192af51d5fec4e00b596f37df2f4e3585f4b5)
Any computer that has this package installed or running should be considered fully compromised. All secrets and keys stored on that computer should be rotated immediately from a different computer. The package should be removed, but as full control of the computer may have been given to an outside entity, there is no guarantee that removing the package will remove all malicious software resulting from installing it.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
